---
title: "HBV Series: Structural Identifiability Analysis"
subtitle: "Which Parameters Can We Estimate from Clinical Measurements?"
author:
  - Vijay Ivaturi
---

:::{.callout-note}
## HBV Tutorial Series

This is **Tutorial 4 of 6** in the HBV series.

| # | Tutorial | Status |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | [Model Introduction](hbv_01_model_introduction_tutorial.qmd) | ✓ Complete |
| 2 | [Copula Vpop Generation](hbv_02_copula_vpop_tutorial.qmd) | ✓ Complete |
| 3 | [Global Sensitivity Analysis](hbv_03_gsa_tutorial.qmd) | ✓ Complete |
| 4 | **Structural Identifiability (current)** | ← You are here |
| 5 | [ILP Calibration](hbv_05_ilp_calibration_tutorial.qmd) | |
| 6 | [VCT Simulation](hbv_06_vct_simulation_tutorial.qmd) | |

:::

## Learning Outcomes

Structural identifiability analysis determines
whether model parameters can be uniquely estimated from available measurements,
assuming perfect data [@wieland2021].

In this tutorial, you will learn:

- The challenges of identifiability in complex QSP models
- How different measurement scenarios affect parameter identifiability
- Practical approaches for the 11-ODE HBV model
- Strategies when parameters are non-identifiable

:::{.callout-tip}
## Prerequisites

This tutorial builds on:

- **[HBV-01](hbv_01_model_introduction_tutorial.qmd)**:
  HBV model structure (22 fixed + 9 estimated params)
- **[TB-04](tb_04_structural_identifiability_tutorial.qmd)**: Detailed SI theory

We'll reference the SI concepts from TB-04 without repeating the full theory.
:::

## Background

### The Identifiability Challenge for HBV

The HBV model is significantly more complex than the tumor burden model:

| Aspect | Tumor Burden | HBV QSP |
|:-------|:-------------|:--------|
| State variables | 2 | 11 |
| Estimated parameters | 3 | 9 |
| Fixed parameters | 0 | 22 |
| Observable outputs | 1 (tumor size) | 2+ (HBsAg, HBV DNA, ALT) |

With 11 coupled ODEs and 9 estimated parameters,
formal global identifiability analysis becomes computationally challenging.
We'll use **measurement scenario analysis** to understand identifiability.

## Libraries

```{julia}
#| output: false
using Pumas
using AlgebraOfGraphics
using CairoMakie
using SummaryTables
```

## Part 1: Measurement Scenarios

In HBV clinical practice, different measurements are available:

### Available Clinical Measurements

```{julia}
# HBV clinical measurements
measurements = DataFrame(
    biomarker = ["HBsAg", "HBV DNA", "ALT", "Infected cells (I)", "Effector cells (E)"],
    routine = ["Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "No (biopsy)", "No (research)"],
    model_variable = ["S (surface antigen)", "V (virus)", "Z (ALT proxy)", "I (infected)", "E (effector)"],
    cost = ["Low", "Moderate", "Low", "High (invasive)", "Very High"]
)

println("HBV Clinical Measurements:")
simple_table(measurements)
```

### Measurement Scenarios

```{julia}
# Define measurement scenarios
scenarios = DataFrame(
    scenario = ["A: Routine", "B: Standard", "C: Enhanced", "D: Research"],
    measurements = ["HBsAg only", "HBsAg + HBV DNA", "HBsAg + HBV DNA + ALT", "All observables"],
    clinical_setting = ["Primary care", "Hepatology clinic", "Clinical trial", "Mechanistic study"]
)

println("\nMeasurement Scenarios:")
simple_table(scenarios)
```

## Part 2: Identifiability by Scenario

Based on the model structure and GSA results, we can reason about identifiability:

### Scenario A: HBsAg Only

When only HBsAg is measured:

```{julia}
scenario_a = DataFrame(
    parameter = ["beta", "p_S", "m", "k_Z", "convE", "epsNUC", "epsIFN", "r_E_IFN", "k_D"],
    identifiable = ["No", "Partially", "No", "No", "No", "Partially", "Partially", "No", "No"],
    reasoning = [
        "Infection process not observed",
        "Affects HBsAg production (indirect)",
        "Killing not directly observed",
        "ALT not measured",
        "Effector dynamics hidden",
        "Treatment effect on S observed",
        "Treatment effect on S observed",
        "Immune boost not observable",
        "DC activation not measured",
    ]
)

println("Scenario A (HBsAg only):")
simple_table(scenario_a)

# Count
n_ident_a = count(x -> x ∈ ["Yes", "Partially"], scenario_a.identifiable)
println("\nIdentifiable/Partially: $n_ident_a of 9 parameters")
```

### Scenario B: HBsAg + HBV DNA

Adding viral load improves identifiability:

```{julia}
scenario_b = DataFrame(
    parameter = ["beta", "p_S", "m", "k_Z", "convE", "epsNUC", "epsIFN", "r_E_IFN", "k_D"],
    identifiable = ["Partially", "Yes", "Partially", "No", "Partially", "Yes", "Yes", "Partially", "No"],
    reasoning = [
        "Infection rate affects V-S relationship",
        "HBsAg baseline directly observed",
        "Killing affects V decline",
        "ALT still not measured",
        "Affects immune-mediated decline",
        "Viral suppression directly observed",
        "Viral suppression observed",
        "Enhances V decline indirectly",
        "DC not observed",
    ]
)

println("Scenario B (HBsAg + HBV DNA):")
simple_table(scenario_b)

n_ident_b = count(x -> x ∈ ["Yes", "Partially"], scenario_b.identifiable)
println("\nIdentifiable/Partially: $n_ident_b of 9 parameters")
```

### Scenario C: Full Clinical Panel

Adding ALT provides inflammation information:

```{julia}
scenario_c = DataFrame(
    parameter = ["beta", "p_S", "m", "k_Z", "convE", "epsNUC", "epsIFN", "r_E_IFN", "k_D"],
    identifiable = ["Partially", "Yes", "Partially", "Yes", "Partially", "Yes", "Yes", "Partially", "Partially"],
    reasoning = [
        "Better constrained by V-S-Z",
        "Directly observed",
        "Affects cell death → ALT",
        "ALT production now observed",
        "Effector-ALT relationship",
        "Direct viral suppression",
        "Direct viral suppression",
        "Immune enhancement in ALT",
        "DC affects immune → ALT",
    ]
)

println("Scenario C (HBsAg + HBV DNA + ALT):")
simple_table(scenario_c)

n_ident_c = count(x -> x ∈ ["Yes", "Partially"], scenario_c.identifiable)
println("\nIdentifiable/Partially: $n_ident_c of 9 parameters")
```

## Part 3: Visualization

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Parameter identifiability across measurement scenarios. Green = identifiable, Yellow = partially identifiable, Red = non-identifiable."

# Create comparison matrix
params = ["beta", "p_S", "m", "k_Z", "convE", "epsNUC", "epsIFN", "r_E_IFN", "k_D"]
scenarios_list = ["A: HBsAg", "B: +DNA", "C: +ALT"]

# Convert to numeric: 0=No, 1=Partial, 2=Yes
function ident_to_num(s)
    s == "Yes" && return 2.0
    s == "Partially" && return 1.0
    return 0.0
end

matrix = zeros(length(params), 3)
matrix[:, 1] = ident_to_num.(scenario_a.identifiable)
matrix[:, 2] = ident_to_num.(scenario_b.identifiable)
matrix[:, 3] = ident_to_num.(scenario_c.identifiable)

fig = Figure(size = (700, 500))
ax = Axis(
    fig[1, 1],
    title = "HBV Parameter Identifiability by Measurement Scenario",
    xlabel = "Measurement Scenario",
    ylabel = "Parameter",
    xticks = (1:3, scenarios_list),
    yticks = (1:9, params)
)

hm = heatmap!(
    ax, 1:3, 1:9, matrix',
    colormap = cgrad([:indianred, :gold, :seagreen], 3, categorical = true),
    colorrange = (0, 2)
)

# Add legend
Legend(
    fig[1, 2],
    [PolyElement(color = c) for c in [:seagreen, :gold, :indianred]],
    ["Identifiable", "Partially", "Non-identifiable"],
    "Status",
    framevisible = false
)

fig
```

## Part 4: Practical Recommendations

### For Standard Clinical Trials (Scenario B)

```{julia}
println("="^50)
println("RECOMMENDATIONS: Standard Clinical Trial (HBsAg + HBV DNA)")
println("="^50)

println("\n✓ Parameters to estimate:")
println("  • p_S - HBsAg production (identifiable from baseline)")
println("  • epsNUC - NUC efficacy (from viral suppression)")
println("  • epsIFN - IFN efficacy (from viral suppression)")

println("\n⚠ Parameters requiring priors:")
println("  • beta - Use informative prior from literature")
println("  • m, convE - Weak identifiability, use priors")
println("  • r_E_IFN - Constrain range from prior studies")

println("\n✗ Parameters to fix:")
println("  • k_Z - Fix to literature value (ALT not measured)")
println("  • k_D - Fix to literature value (DC not observable)")
```

### Improving Identifiability

```{julia}
# Strategies to improve identifiability
strategies = DataFrame(
    strategy = [
        "Add ALT measurement",
        "Use informative priors",
        "Fix non-influential params",
        "Hierarchical modeling",
        "Sequential design",
    ],
    parameters_affected = [
        "k_Z, m, convE",
        "beta, k_D",
        "k_Z, k_D (from GSA)",
        "All with IIV",
        "Focus on unknowns",
    ],
    implementation = [
        "Standard lab test, low cost",
        "From meta-analysis",
        "Simplify model",
        "Population approach",
        "Adaptive trial design",
    ]
)

println("\nStrategies to Improve Identifiability:")
simple_table(strategies)
```

## Part 5: Integration with GSA

Combining GSA results (from Tutorial 3) with identifiability:

```{julia}
# Combined GSA + SI recommendations
combined = DataFrame(
    parameter = params,
    gsa_influence = ["Low", "High", "Moderate", "Low", "Moderate", "High", "High", "Moderate", "Low"],
    si_status_B = scenario_b.identifiable,
    recommendation = [
        "Fix to literature",
        "Estimate (key driver)",
        "Use informative prior",
        "Fix (not measured)",
        "Use informative prior",
        "Estimate (key driver)",
        "Estimate (key driver)",
        "Use informative prior",
        "Fix to literature",
    ]
)

println("Combined GSA + Identifiability Recommendations:")
simple_table(combined)
```

```{julia}
#| fig-cap: "Parameter importance (GSA) vs identifiability. Ideal parameters are in the top-right (influential and identifiable)."

# Create DataFrame for AoG plotting
influence_scores = [0.05, 0.35, 0.15, 0.03, 0.12, 0.3, 0.28, 0.18, 0.04]  # From GSA
ident_scores = ident_to_num.(scenario_b.identifiable)

priority_df = DataFrame(
    parameter = params,
    gsa_influence = influence_scores,
    identifiability = ident_scores,
    action = combined.recommendation
)

# Create figure with AoG
fig = Figure(size = (800, 500))
ax = Axis(
    fig[1, 1],
    title = "Parameter Priority Matrix",
    xlabel = "GSA Influence (Sₜ)",
    ylabel = "Identifiability Score",
    yticks = ([0, 1, 2], ["Non-identifiable", "Partial", "Identifiable"])
)

# AoG scatter plot
plt = data(priority_df) * mapping(:gsa_influence, :identifiability, color = :action) * visual(Scatter, markersize = 20)
draw!(ax, plt, scales(Color = (; palette = [:green, :red, :orange])))

# Add parameter labels (Makie - AoG doesn't support text annotations)
for row in eachrow(priority_df)
    text!(
        ax, row.gsa_influence + 0.01, row.identifiability + 0.1,
        text = row.parameter, fontsize = 10
    )
end

# Reference lines (Makie - AoG doesn't support hlines/vlines)
vlines!(ax, [0.1], color = :gray, linestyle = :dash)
hlines!(ax, [1.5], color = :gray, linestyle = :dash)

# Quadrant labels (Makie - AoG doesn't support positioned text)
text!(ax, 0.25, 1.8, text = "Priority:\nEstimate", fontsize = 12, color = :green)
text!(ax, 0.02, 1.8, text = "Use\nPriors", fontsize = 12, color = :orange)
text!(ax, 0.02, 0.2, text = "Fix", fontsize = 12, color = :red)

# Add legend outside the plot
Legend(
    fig[1, 2],
    [MarkerElement(marker = :circle, color = c, markersize = 15) for c in [:green, :orange, :red]],
    ["Estimate (key driver)", "Use informative prior", "Fix to literature"],
    "Action",
    framevisible = false
)

fig
```

## Summary

### Key Findings

| Scenario | Measurements | Identifiable Params | Recommendation |
|:---------|:-------------|:--------------------|:---------------|
| A | HBsAg only | 3 | Insufficient for estimation |
| B | HBsAg + DNA | 6 | Standard trial minimum |
| C | + ALT | 8 | Best for full characterization |

### Parameter Classification

**Estimate (identifiable + influential):**

- p_S, epsNUC, epsIFN

**Use priors (partially identifiable or low influence):**

- beta, m, convE, r_E_IFN

**Fix (non-identifiable and low influence):**

- k_Z, k_D

### What's Next?

In [Tutorial 5: ILP Calibration](hbv_05_ilp_calibration_tutorial.qmd),
we'll calibrate the HBV virtual population to match clinical trial baseline characteristics.

### References

::: {#refs}
:::
